name: He that receiveth you receiveth me
notes: Three Gospel records of the same saying of Christ: to receive (or hear) his sent messengers is to receive him, and to receive him is to receive the Father who sent him. Matthew and John are near-identical, sharing the exact chain "he that receiveth me receiveth him that sent me" and casting the messengers' reception as Christ's own (Matthew: "He that receiveth you"; John: "He that receiveth whomsoever I send"). Luke records the saying from a complementary vantage, using hearing and despising rather than receiving, yet binds the same chain to the Father: "he that despiseth me despiseth him that sent me." The shared core is the unbroken identification of messenger with Master and Master with Sender; Luke's added clause on despising preserves the warning side of the same truth, which is left as a matter for further study.
